State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Toll Free (In State) • • • Administration • • • Legal • • • Enforcement • • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662        322-5660        323-3901        322-6441        322-6444

October 20, 1983


Jan Schori
Sacramento Municipal Utility
    District
6201 S Street
P.O. Box 18530
Sacramento, CA  95813

                                        Re:  Advice Letter No. A-83-201

Dear Ms. Schori:

     I apologize for the delay in my response to you.  You wrote
to request advice on behalf of Mr. John Kehoe, the President of
the Sacramento Municipal Utility District's (SMUD) Board of
Directors.  The facts, as stated in your letter, are that
Mr. Kehoe is the Vice-President of Cook Company, which engages
in real estate development.  In August, Cook Company submitted a
proposal to McDonnell-Douglas to purchase 4,000 acres of its
property.1/  This property is in an area of the City of Folsom
which was recently annexed by SMUD.  The annexation is complete,
except that service to the area has not yet commenced.

     Prior to beginning service, there are several items which
must be considered by the SMUD Board.  Mr. Kehoe has asked
whether the conflict of interest provisions of the Political
Reform Act require his disqualification on these matters.  The
items in question are:

     1.  Mather Air Force Base has requested detachment from
SMUD.  The request was referred to the Local Agency Formation
Commission and PG&E is opposing the detachment.  Additional SMUD Board action may be required.

_______________________
1/  McDonnell-Douglas is trying to rezone this land from
agricultural to heavy industrial.

Jan Schori
October 20, 1983
Page 2


     2.  The SMUD Board must determine how to allocate a
surcharge amount among the customers in the annexed area.
The surcharge amount is intended to recover the cost of
extending service to the new area, plus an amount equivalent
to the existing SMUD customers' equity ownership in SMUD.
The Board may decide to impose the surcharge equally on all
customer classes (i.e., residential, commercial, industrial,
or agricultural), or to differentiate between classes.

     3.  PG&E and SMUD are negotiating the value to be paid
for PG&E's electrical facilities annexed in the area.  The
SMUD Board will either approve a settlement amount or it
will authorize a condemnation action.  The amount paid will
be passed on to the consumer through the surcharge.

     4.  A consulting panel for the Folsom annexation was
established by the SMUD Board.  Mr. Kehoe is the chairman
and he appointed the other members.  To date, the group has
not been very active, but its stated goals are to promote
communication between the elected SMUD Board, the people of
Folsom and the SMUD ratepayers.  The group will remain well
informed on the key points of the annexation; it will
communicate details concerning the service and costs to the
people of Folsom; and it hopes to expedite the public
process involved in obtaining any permits that are necessary
to provide the electrical service.  You stated that it is
possible that, at some time, the group may appear before the
SMUD Board of Directors to support or oppose a matter before
the Board.

                              DISCUSSION

     Government Code Section 87100 2/ prohibits a local public
official from making, participating in the making, or in any way
attempting to use his official position to influence a govern-
mental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a
financial interest.

     An official has a financial interest in a decision
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material

___________________
     2/  Hereinafter all statutory references are to the
Government Code, unless otherwise indicated.

Jan Schori
October 20, 1983
Page 3


     financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on
the public generally, on:

                              * * *

     (c)  Any source of income . . . aggregating two
hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value provided
to, . . . the public official within 12 months prior to
the time when the decision is made.

     (d)  Any business entity in which the public
official is an . . . officer . . . .

                          Section 87103.

     Cook Company is a source of income to Mr. Kehoe and he is an
officer in the company.  With regard to the matters outlined
above, Mr. Kehoe must disqualify himself from a decision if (1)
it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect on Cook Company, and (2) the effect of
the decision on the company will be distinguishable from its
effect on the public generally.  (See 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
18703.)

     A decision will have a "material" effect on Cook Company if
the effect of the decision will be to increase or decrease:

          (A)  The annualized gross revenues by the lesser
of:

               1.  One hundred thousand dollars ($100,000);

or

               2.  One percent if the effect is one thousand
dollars ($1,000) or more; or

          (B)  Annual net income by the lesser of:

               1.  Fifty thousand dollars ($50,000); or

               2.  One half of one percent if the effect is
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more; or

          (C)  Current assets or liabilities by the lesser
of:

Jan Schori
October 20, 1983
Page 4


                    1.  One hundred thousand dollars ($100,000);
         or

                    2.  One half of one percent if the effect is
         one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

                             (2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
                             18702(b)(1)(A), (B), (C).)

     If the decision will have a material effect on Cook Company,
Mr. Kehoe is allowed to make or participate in the decision if
the effect on the company is substantially similar to the effect
on the public generally or a significant segment of the public
generally.  In this case, the "public generally" is the entire
SMUD service area.

     In requesting advice on this matter, you have asked us to
assume that it is reasonably foreseeable that Cook Company will
purchase the McDonnell-Douglas property.

Mather Air Force Base Detachment:

     If, as you stated in your letter, the detachment of Mather
Air Force Base from SMUD will have little or no impact on any
other customer, then it is not foreseeable that decisions
concerning the detachment will have a material financial effect
on Cook Company.  Therefore Mr. Kehoe is not required to
disqualify himself from decisions on the detachment.

Surcharge:

     The SMUD staff will determine how much money must be
recovered by the surcharge and the SMUD Board of Directors will
determine how to allocate the amount among the various users in
the annexation area.  The Board may choose to distribute the
surcharge evenly among all of the users in the annexation area,
or it may differentiate between the various classes of users.
The following method should be used by Mr. Kehoe to determine
whether he must disqualify himself from choosing between the
methods of allocation being considered.

     Mr. Kehoe must separately determine the amount of surcharge
that Cook Company will foreseeably pay under both the least and
the most financially burdensome methods being considered.  If
the difference between the two amounts will foreseeably have a
material financial effect on Cook Company, he must disqualify
himself.  In order to determine whether the amount will be

Jan Schori
October 20, 1983
Page 5


material, refer to the test stated in 2 California
Administrative Code Section 18702(b)(1). (See page 3.)

     If the decision will have a material effect on Cook Company,
the effect will be distinguishable from the effect on all, or a
significant segment, of the public generally.  Under any method
of allocation, the largest group which could be similarly
affected is all of the users in the annexation area.  The group
does not constitute a significant segment of the public.3/

Decision on the Purchase of the PG&E Facilities:

     As part of the annexation, SMUD will purchase the existing
PG&E facilities in the area.  If a settlement with PG&E can be
reached, the SMUD Board will be asked to approve the settlement
amount.  If there is no agreement, or if the SMUD Board does not
approve the settlement amount, it may authorize condemnation
proceedings.  The amount paid for the facilities will be
included in the surcharge.  Mr. Kehoe must disqualify himself if
the decision to accept or reject the settlement amount will
foreseeably have a material financial effect on Cook
Company.4/

_________________

     3/  The approximate figures for users inside and outside
of the annexation area are as follows:

                                    Inside      Outside     Percentage

     Small commercial users         850       28,000          3%
     Large commercial/industrial     43        5,000      less than 1%
     Residential                  3,900      313,000          1%
     Agricultural                    1        2,800      less than 1%
     Street and traffic lights       4        1,172      less than 1%
     Outdoor lights                  0        8,500          0%
     
          Total Users             4,798      358,672          1%

     4/  In determining whether the decision will foreseeably have
a material effect on Cook Company, it may be helpful to consider
the difference between the settlement amount and the amount that
reasonably can be expected to be paid after a condemnation action.

Jan Schori
October 20, 1983
Page 6


Consulting Panel:

     Mr. Kehoe is the chairman of the consulting panel that was
established by the SMUD Board.  He appointed the other members of
the panel.  The primary purpose of the panel is to present
information to the public concerning the annexation.  If this is
the extent of the panel's activities, Mr. Kehoe will be able to
fully participate.  However, if the panel engages in decision
making, the disqualification provisions of the Act will apply.  The
consulting panel has decision making authority if:

     (A)  It may make a final governmental decision;

     (B)  It may compel a governmental decision; or it
may prevent a governmental decision either by reason of
an exclusive power to initiate the decision or by
reason of a veto which may not be overridden; or

     (C)  It makes substantive recommendations which
are, and over an extended period of time have been,
regularly approved without significant amendment or
modification by another public official or governmental
agency.

                    (2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
                    18700(a)(1)(A), (B), (C).)


     Even if the panel has no decision making authority, there
may be circumstances in which Mr. Kehoe must disqualify himself
from acting as a member of the panel.  You stated that it is
possible that the consulting panel will appear before the SMUD
Board to make recommendations on important decisions.  The Act
prohibits Mr. Kehoe from furthering or attempting to affect in
any manner any decision within or before the SMUD Board which
will foreseeably have a material effect on Cook Company.
(Section 87100 and 2 California Administrative Code Section
18702(e).)  Thus, as a member of the SMUD Board, Mr. Kehoe is
prohibited from advocating the consulting panel's position
before the Board, or preparing the panel's presentation if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision that they are
attempting to influence will have a material financial effect on
Cook Company.

Jan Schori
October 20, 1983
Page 7


     I hope that I have answered all of your questions for you.
If I can be of any help to you in the future, please feel free
to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

                                   Very truly yours,

                                   [signature]
                                   Janis Shank McLean
                                   Counsel
                                   Legal Division

JSM:plh

SMUD
SACRAMENTO MUNICIPAL UTILITY DISTRICT □ 6201 S Street, P.O. Box 15830, Sacramento, CA 95813; (916) 452-3211
AN ELECTRIC SYSTEM SERVING THE HEART OF CALIFORNIA


August 29, 1983


Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento CA 95814

RE: Request for Opinion
Pursuant to Government Code
Section 83114


At the SMUD board meeting August 18, 1983,

Michael Krug from the Sacramento Campaign for Economic

Democracy referred to the attached article from the Sacramento

Bee regarding the sale of some property owned by McDonnell-

Douglas in the area of Folsom recently annexed by SMUD. The

article indicated that the Cook Company is the "likely buyer"

of the property. The President of the SMUD Board of Directors

is John Kehoe. Mr. Kehoe is Vice President of the Cook

Company. Mr. Krug requested that the Board seek an opinion

from the Fair Political Practices Commission on whether

Mr. Kehoe has a conflict of interest when participating in

decisions relating to the Folsom annexation because of the Cook

Company's possible purchase of the McDonnell-Douglas acreage.

Mr. Krug's letter refers to a second parcel of

property -- the Cook Company development along White Rock

Fair Political Practices Commission
August 29, 1983
Re: Request for Opinion

-2-

Road.  That property is within the existing SMUD service area
and was not part of the Folsom annexation.  It need not be con-
sidered here.

     The Board members, including Mr. Kehoe, agreed to seek
such an opinion.  A copy of Mr. Krug's statement is attached.
This request for opinion is submitted on Mr. Kehoe's behalf
pursuant to the provisions of Government Code Section 83114.

     The Folsom annexation is complete although service to
the area has not yet commenced.  The following major items
remain for possible Board action and/or involvement:

     1.   Mather Air Force Base has requested detachment
from SMUD.  The request has been referred to the Local
Agency Formation Commission.  PGandE is opposing the
detachment; further SMUD Board action may be necessary.

     2.   PGandE and SMUD are negotiating the value to be
paid for the electrical facilities in the annexed
area.  The SMUD Board will either approve a settlement
or authorize condemnation proceedings in order for the
District to commence service.

     3.   The SMUD Board must determine the surcharge
amount to be imposed upon customers in the annexed
area.  The surcharge is intended to recover the Dis-
trict's cost of extending service to the new area as
well as an amount equivalent to existing SMUD cus-
tomers' equity ownership in SMUD.  The surcharge may
be imposed on all customer classes (i.e., residential,
commercial, industrial, agricultural), or may
differentiate between classes.

     4.   An advisory committee was established by the
Board on April 21, 1983.  A copy of the resolution
establishing the advisory committee is attached,
including a statement of the committee's goals,

Fair Political Practices Commission
August 29, 1983
Re: Request for Opinion

-3-

objectives, and membership. Mr. Kehoe is chairman of
the group, and its members were appointed by him. To
date, the group has had one organizational meeting.
Mr. Kehoe plans to call one meeting per quarter for
the next two years.

A few additional facts may be helpful in making your
analysis. The McDonnell-Douglas property is currently vacant
and zoned agricultural. McDonnell-Douglas is attempting to
rezone the property for heavy industrial development.
Generally in the SMUD service area the tenants in commercial or
industrial developments are SMUD customers, not the developer.
In some cases the developer remains SMUD's customer and elec-
tricity costs are included in tenants' rent.

If we assume it is reasonably foreseeable that the Cook
Company will purchase the McDonnell-Douglas property, the ques-
tion is whether SMUD Board or advisory committee action on any
of the above items will have a material financial effect upon
the Cook Company distinguishable from its effect on a signi-
ficant segment of the public.

We have not been able to develop scenarios or circum-
stances where the Cook Company's interest is severable from
that of the public generally with respect to Items 1 and 2, the
Mather detachment and the acquisition of PGandE's facilities.
The Mather detachment should have little or no impact on any
other customer, and it seems to us that all customers in the

Fair Political Practices Commission
August 29, 1983
Re: Request for Opinion

-4-

annexed area have the same interest in securing as low a price

as possible for the PGandE facilities.

The surcharge presents a more difficult problem.

While we do not yet know how large a sum will have to be col-

lected or in what manner the Board may consider allocating that

sum among the various customer classes, it is possible to con-

struct a "worst case" scenario under which a Board decision on

allocation might affect the market value of the McDonnell-

Douglas property by an amount greater than that set forth in

Section 18702(b)(2) of your regulations. One would then have

to consider whether that decision would affect the owner of the

McDonnell-Douglas property in a different manner than it would

affect a significant segment of the public.

We have discussed these matters with Mr. Kehoe. He

has advised us that as long as the Cook Company is seriously

considering purchase of the McDonnell-Douglas property he does

not intend to participate in any decision relating to the sur-

charge that would not be in accord with the principles outlined

in the preceding paragraph.

We would appreciate your opinion and any additional

advice you may have regarding Mr. Kehoe's continued involvement

as a board member in the matters relating to the Folsom annexa-

tion.

Fair Political Practices Commission
August 29, 1983
Re: Request for Opinion

-5-

Please call me if you have questions. Thank you for
your assistance.

Sincerely,

Jan Schori
Attorney

cc: Board Members
J. Mattimoe
D. Kaplan

Sacramento Campaign for Economic Democracy
Statement to SMUD Board of Directors

August 18, 1983


Members of the Board:

My name is Michael Krug. I am here as a rate payer and part owner of
SMUD and as a representative of the Sacramento Chapter of The Campaign
for Economic Democracy.

I am addressing the Board today because of a concern that there is a
possibility of a conflict of interests; specifically in matters concerning Director Kehoe as the Vice President of the Cook Company and certain
properties in the newly annexed area.

Let me refer to an article that appeared in the Sacramento Bee on August
10th. The article explains that McDonald Douglas is trying to sell 4,000
acres of it's property in southeast Sacramento County and this article
also identifies the Cook Company as the "likely buyer".

Since John Kehoe is the President of SMUD, he sits on the Annexation
Advisory Committee, and he is Vice President of a company engaged in
substantial business dealings in the annexed area, we suspect that there
is a question of a conflict of interests.

According to the Fair Political Practices Committee, a conflict of interests
occurs on these two points. First, when a Public Official is affiliated
with (quote) "Any business entity which the Public Official is a Director,
Officer, Partner, Trustee, Employee or holds any position of management",
and secondly, when that elected person makes a decision as a Public Official
and, (I quote again) "It is reasonably forseeable that the decision will
have a material financial effect" upon the official or his company.

It may be "reasonably forseeable" that the value of this land has been
enhanced by it's annexation into SMUD, as may have other properties or
developments tied to the Cook Company in this area, such as the development
along White Rock Road.

Neither I nor the SCED is accusing Mr. Kehoe of any wrongdoing. But as
concerned citizens we feel it is our duty to request an answer to the
questions that are raised by Mr. Kehoe's position on the SMUD Board and on
the Annexation Advisory Committee and his employment as Vice President
of the Cook Company.

The SCED requests that SMUD asks the FPPC for an opinion in this matter.
The SCED also requests that Mr. Kehoe abstain from any vote concerning the
annexed area until the FPPC delivers it's opinion. This will require a
minimum of time as the FPPC is obligated to deliver an opinion within 21
working days.

Thank You,

Michael Krug
SCED

Business

The Sacramento Bee Wednesday, August 10, 1983

M'Donnell 4,000 Acres Near Sale

By Jack Strnad
Bee Staff Writer

McDonnell Douglas Corp.'s 4,000-
acre southwest of Airport are on
the market and likely to be sold
within days, industry sources told
The Bee Tuesday.

Robert Young, executive vice
president of MJC Realty In Irvine,
which has the listing for the
McDonnell Douglas land, said his
firm has received several unsolicited
proposals to purchase the land.

He said the property could be
developed as either a new town,
rezoned from agricultural to heavy
industrial, or a combination of both.

Improved property within three
miles of the area — at Highway 50
and County Road — is appraising as
high as $200,000 an acre.

"We have had four proposals for
the property, including three from
major developers and two major
holdings of Sacramento," Young
said. He added that some of the pro-
spective buyers are willing to buy
the property before it is rezoned.

The MJC executive said his firm
is "very close to an agreement with
one of the developers," and "we hope
to be in Sacramento in a few days to
advance the agreement."

The property is located just east
of Sun Isle Boulevard and is bounded
on the north by the American River
and on the south by Douglas Road.

Real estate sources in the area
have told The Bee that the likely
buyer is the Uco Co., which current-
ly is developing the 7,000 acre site
by White Inc. project along the

Highway 50 corridor.

John Kelso, Uco Co. vice presi-
dent, told The Bee that the county's
just-completed environmental
impact report on the Uco project
has not made an offer.

"We have been working with the
property owner on the project, but
based on all the reports, it is too early
to make a statement," Kelso said.

Young said the 4,000 acres is "too
large of a property for McDonnell
Douglas to keep," and said the real
estate firm prefers to develop much smaller parcels such as its 50 to 200 acre
projects in Irvine, Cypress and
Huntington Beach.

McDonnell Douglas bought the property
zoned for the last two years.

The acquisition of the property has been scrutinized as Security
Park, a 330-acre industrial project.

and Space Administration.
The firm ceased most of its operations as the aerospace industry slumped in the
1970s. It later put the land under a
Williamson Act contract, a state law
designed to preserve farm land for agricultural uses. But the state recently
canceled the contract in 1981 at the
request of McDonnell Douglas in
anticipation of pursuing a change in
zoning to heavy industrial development.

Young sold his firm has been in
the process of getting the land re-zoned for the last two years.

The acquisition of the property
has been scrutinized as Security
Park, a 330-acre industrial project.

RESOLUTION NO. 83-4-25

BE IT RESOLVED BY THE BOARD OF DIRECTORS
OF SACRAMENTO MUNICIPAL UTILITY DISTRICT:

A Folsom Annexation Consultative Group shall

be established with the goal, objectives, and membership

as outlined on the attached sheet. The members of the

group shall be appointed by the president of the SMUD

Board.


Adopted April 21, 1983

INTRODUCED BY DIRECTOR _______________
SECONDED BY DIRECTOR _______________
DIRECTOR AYE NO ABSTAIN ABSENT
WILCOX | ✓
TAYLOR | ✓
CARR | ✓
CASTRO | ✓
KEHOE | ✓

PROPOSAL - Create a seven member Consultative Panel to the

SMUD Board for the Folsom Annexation.


GOAL - Create a consultative group to bridge the communicatio

process between the elected SMUD Board, the people of Folsom

and the SMUD ratepayers.


OBJECTIVES

1. Review process relating to the District's program to ren

service to the annexed area on a periodical basis to be well

informed on the key points of the annexation.


2. Communicate key elements to the people of Folsom so they

will be fully informed on timing for service, cost expectations

of PG&E properties for the purchase of its system by SMUD.


3. Expedite the public process relating to the permit steps

necessary to render electric service on a timely basis.


4. Nature of panel's membership should measurably assist in

this achievement.


MEMBERSHIP - Member Board of Supervisors, Folsom City Council

member, Sacramento City Council, Representative of Central

Labor Council, Sacramento Metropolitan Chamber of Commerce,

Urban League and the President of the SMUD Board who will

chair the group.

Business                                                                                                                                    C

The Sacramento Bee   Wednesday, August 10, 1983

M'Donnell 4,000 Acres Near Sale

By Jack Sirard
Sacramento Bee Staff Writer

McDonnell Douglas Corp.'s 4,000
acres southwest of Aerojet are on
the market and could be sold
within days, industry sources told
The Bee Tuesday.

Robert Young, executive vice
president of MDC Realty in Irvine,
confirmed the development arm of
McDonnell Douglas said his firm
has been fielding inquiries about
proposals to purchase the land.

He said the property could be
valued at $140-450 million if it were
assessed on agricultural or heavy
industrial uses, but probably could
be developed property within three
miles of the area — at Highway 50
and Zinfandel Drive — is selling for
as high as $200,000 an acre.

"We have had four proposals for
the property, including three from
major corporations and one from
kings of Sacramento," Young
said. He added that none of the potential buyers are willing to buy the
property before it is rezoned.

The MDC executive said his firm
is "very close to an agreement with
one of the buyers," but declined
to say which buyer it is Sacramento
to advise the agreement.

The property is located just east
of Sun-rise Boulevard and is bounded
on the north by White Rock Road
and on the south by Douglas Road.

Sources have told The Bee that the likely
buyer is the Cook Co. which currently is developing 117 acres at its nearby White Rock project along

Highway 50 corridor.

John Kimoto, Cook Co. vice president, said the firm is hunting the
county's just-completed environmental impact report on the property but
has not made an offer.

"We have been working with the
county and with MDC on the report, but it's too early to make a judgment yet," Kimoto said.

The Cook Co. expects to be in Sacramento in a few days to advise the agreement.

The property is located just east
of Sunrise Boulevard and is bounded
on the north by White Rock Road
and on the south by Douglas Road.

Young said the 4,000 acres is "too
large of a property for McDonnell
Douglas to build rockets on," and
that his firm prefers to develop much smaller parcels such as its 50 to 70 acre
projects in Irvine, Cypress and
Huntington Beach.

The property, sold to McDonnell
Douglas by Aerojet more than 20
years ago, has been used as a test site for
McDonnell Douglas rockets produced for the National Aeronautics

and Space Administration.

"The firm ceased most of its operations at the site several years ago when
the space industry slumped in the
1970s. It later put it in a kind under a
Williamson Act contract, a state law
designed to preserve farm property
by offering the owner a tax break.

The county would have to
cancel the contract in 1981 at the
request of McDonnell Douglas in
anticipation of pursuing a change in
zoning to heavy industrial development.

Young said his firm has been in
escrow of selling the land and has
zoned for the last two years.

The southeast corner of the property has been developed as security
Park, a 30-acre industrial project.

September 1, 1983


Jan Schori
Attorney
Sacramento Municipal Utility
District
P.O. Box 15830
Sacramento, CA  95813
                                                Re: A-83-201

Dear Ms. Schori:

     Your letter requesting advice under the Political
Reform Act has been referred to me.  If you have any
questions about your advice request, please contact me
directly at (916) 322-5901.

     We try to answer all advice requests promptly.
Therefore, unless your request poses particularly complex
legal questions, or unless more information is needed to
answer your request, you should expect a response within 21
working days.

                                        Very truly yours,


                                        Barbara A. Milman
                                        Chief
                                        Legal Division

PROPOSAL - Create a seven member Consultative Panel to the 
SMUD Board for the Folsom Annexation. 
GOAL - Create a consultative group to bridge the communicatic 
process between the elected SMUD Board, the people of Folsom 
and the S~IUD ratepayers. 
OBJECTIVES 
1. 
Review process relating to the District's program to rende 
service to the annexed area on a periodical basis to be well 
informed on the key points of the annexation. 
2. 
Communicate key elements to the people of Folsom so they 
will be fully informed on timing for service, cost expectations 
of PG&E properties for the purchase of its system by SMUD. 
3. 
Expedite the public process relating to the permit steps 
necessary to render electric service on a timely b~sis. 
4. 
~ature of panel's membership should measurably assist in 
this achievement.

member, Sacramento City Council, Representative of Central 
Labor Council, Sacramento Metropolitan Chamber of Commerce, 
Urban League and the President of the SHUD Board who will 
chair the group.

sections.conclusion: Mather Air Force Base Detachment: If the detachment will have little or no impact on any other customer, Mr. Kehoe is not required to disqualify himself. Surcharge: Mr. Kehoe must separately determine the surcharge Cook Company will pay under both the least and most financially burdensome allocation methods and disqualify himself only if the difference has a material financial effect on Cook Company. PG&E Facilities: Mr. Kehoe must disqualify himself if the decision on the settlement amount will foreseeably have a material financial effect on Cook Company. Consulting Panel: If the panel's primary purpose is to present information to the public, Mr. Kehoe may fully participate. However, if the panel engages in decision-making with the authority specified in 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700(a)(1), disqualification provisions apply. Mr. Kehoe is prohibited from advocating the panel's position before the Board if the decision will foreseeably have a material financial effect on Cook Company.
sections.analysis:
Government Code Section 871001/ prohibits a local public 
official from making, participating in the making, or in any way 
attempting to use his official position to influence a 
govern-
mental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a 
financial interest. 
An official has a financial interest in a decision 
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the decision will have a material 
~/ Hereinafter Jan Schori 
October 20, 1983  
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on 
the public generally, on:
sections.question_synthetic: Must John Kehoe, President of SMUD's Board and Vice-President of Cook Company (a real estate developer with a proposal to purchase McDonnell-Douglas property in the annexed Folsom area), disqualify himself from SMUD Board decisions regarding the Mather Air Force Base detachment, surcharge allocation, PG&E facility purchase valuation, and the consulting panel on annexation matters?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Mr. Kehoe's disqualification requirements depend on whether SMUD Board decisions will foreseeably have material financial effects on Cook Company, determined by specific materiality thresholds. He need not disqualify himself from the Mather detachment decision, but must analyze the surcharge allocation and PG&E purchase decisions using the material effect test. Regarding the consulting panel, he may participate if it only provides information, but must disqualify himself from advocating its recommendations if they could materially affect Cook Company.
sections.parsing_notes: Mixed formats: ocr, old; LLM: Document contains OCR artifacts and mixed formatting from 1983 era. Primary FPPC response letter is clear with distinct Question, Discussion, and Conclusion sections. Attached incoming request letter from SMUD (August 29, 1983) is partially truncated and heavily degraded by OCR but provides context. All key FPPC advice is extractable and coherent.

QUESTION: s that are raised by Mr. Kehoe's position on the SMUD Board and on 
the Annexation Advisory Committee and his employment as Vice President 
of the Cook Company. 
The SCED requests that SMUD asks the FPPC for an opinion in this matter. 
The SCED also requests that Mr. Kehoe abstain from any vote concerning the 
annexed area until the FPPC delivers it's opinion. 
This will require a 
minim~ of time as the FPPC is obligated to deliver an opinion within 21 
working days.

CONCLUSION: Mather Air Force Base Detachment: If the detachment will have little or no impact on any other customer, Mr. Kehoe is not required to disqualify himself. Surcharge: Mr. Kehoe must separately determine the surcharge Cook Company will pay under both the least and most financially burdensome allocation methods and disqualify himself only if the difference has a material financial effect on Cook Company. PG&E Facilities: Mr. Kehoe must disqualify himself if the decision on the settlement amount will foreseeably have a material financial effect on Cook Company. Consulting Panel: If the panel's primary purpose is to present information to the public, Mr. Kehoe may fully participate. However, if the panel engages in decision-making with the authority specified in 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700(a)(1), disqualification provisions apply. Mr. Kehoe is prohibited from advocating the panel's position before the Board if the decision will foreseeably have a material financial effect on Cook Company.
embedding.first_500_words: State of California Fair Political Practices Commission P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 Toll Free (In State) • • • Administration • • • Legal • • • Enforcement • • • Statements of Economic Interest (916) 322-5662 322-5660 323-3901 322-6441 322-6444 October 20, 1983 Jan Schori Sacramento Municipal Utility District 6201 S Street P.O. Box 18530 Sacramento, CA 95813 Re: Advice Letter No. A-83-201 Dear Ms. Schori: I apologize for the delay in my response to you. You wrote to request advice on behalf of Mr. John Kehoe, the President of the Sacramento Municipal Utility District's (SMUD) Board of Directors. The facts, as stated in your letter, are that Mr. Kehoe is the Vice-President of Cook Company, which engages in real estate development. In August, Cook Company submitted a proposal to McDonnell-Douglas to purchase 4,000 acres of its property.1/ This property is in an area of the City of Folsom which was recently annexed by SMUD. The annexation is complete, except that service to the area has not yet commenced. Prior to beginning service, there are several items which must be considered by the SMUD Board. Mr. Kehoe has asked whether the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act require his disqualification on these matters. The items in question are: 1. Mather Air Force Base has requested detachment from SMUD. The request was referred to the Local Agency Formation Commission and PG&E is opposing the detachment. Additional SMUD Board action may be required. _______________________ 1/ McDonnell-Douglas is trying to rezone this land from agricultural to heavy industrial. Jan Schori October 20, 1983 Page 2 2. The SMUD Board must determine how to allocate a surcharge amount among the customers in the annexed area. The surcharge amount is intended to recover the cost of extending service to the new area, plus an amount equivalent to the existing SMUD customers' equity ownership in SMUD. The Board may decide to impose the surcharge equally on all customer classes (i.e., residential, commercial, industrial, or agricultural), or to differentiate between classes. 3. PG&E and SMUD are negotiating the value to be paid for PG&E's electrical facilities annexed in the area. The SMUD Board will either approve a settlement amount or it will authorize a condemnation action. The amount paid will be passed on to the consumer through the surcharge. 4. A consulting panel for the Folsom annexation was established by the SMUD Board. Mr. Kehoe is the chairman and he appointed the other members. To date, the group has not been very active, but its stated goals are to promote communication between the elected SMUD Board, the people of Folsom and the SMUD ratepayers. The group will remain well informed on the key points of the annexation; it will communicate details concerning the service and costs to the people of Folsom; and it hopes to expedite the public process involved in obtaining any permits that are necessary to provide the electrical service. You
embedding.summary: FPPC Advice Letter A-83-201 addresses whether John Kehoe, SMUD Board President and Vice-President of Cook Company, must disqualify himself from four categories of SMUD Board decisions regarding the Folsom annexation, based on conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act. The FPPC provides specific guidance on materiality thresholds and decision-making authority to determine disqualification requirements for each matter.
